the book arc

Copy it to author a real book.

books.title: Angular 22 Architecture for a Component Library
key_concepts: standalone-only public API
zoneless-compatible OnPush signal-driven components
consumer-owns-the-context
Material/Aria/CDK reach-for ladder
library + demo-app workspace
Angular lockstep
DI for behavior / CSS tokens for design
books.summary: The Angular 22 platform foundations for a component library: standalone components as the unit of distribution; zoneless + OnPush change detection a library can rely on (and the silent-failure trap if it can't); signals and hierarchical DI as the reactive/wiring spine; the Material + Angular Aria + CDK three-layer first-party stack and the reach-for ladder; the lib+demo-app workspace shape (Caelum + Forge); and version lockstep. Version specifics are grounded in research/notes/angular-22-platform.md.

books.title: Reactivity & Forms in Angular 22
key_concepts: build controls on ControlValueAccessor
forms-API-agnostic control
derive with computed not effect
signal component IO
value fidelity / touched / disabled contract
mat-form-field integration
signals vs observables boundary
books.summary: Reactivity and forms for a component library: signals (signal/computed/effect/linkedSignal/untracked) and the rules that keep them honest; signal-based component IO (input/output/model/queries); reactive forms (FormControl/FormGroup/Validators); and ControlValueAccessor as the stable, forms-API-agnostic bridge every Caelum control implements so it works under classic reactive forms AND Signal Forms. Version specifics grounded in research/notes/angular-22-platform.md.

books.title: Provenance & Licensing Discipline
key_concepts: the admit/reject gate (5 checks)
free is not permissive
source-available is not open (the PrimeUI trap)
the rule is on the transitive tree not the package
license is CI-mechanical / origin is human-and-on-record
runtime vs dev dependency strictness
the admit/reject ledger (PROVENANCE.md)
the cheapest dependency is the one you don't add
books.summary: The discipline behind Caelum's two hard rules (no paid license; US-origin incl. the full transitive tree): the license taxonomy (permissive ADMIT vs copyleft/source-available/none REJECT — free is not permissive, and PrimeUI-style source-available is the trap), the five-check admit/reject gate (necessity, license, origin, transitive, isolation), auditing the transitive tree with npm ls + a license scan, the runtime-vs-dev split, continuous CI enforcement + lockfile discipline + re-vet-on-upgrade, and the admit/reject ledger. License is machine-checkable; origin needs a human. Grounds D-05/D-10; stack provenance facts grounded in research/notes/angular-22-platform.md.

books.title: The Theming Token Bridge
key_concepts: one semantic --cae-* token set as the single source of design truth
bind Material's --mat-sys-* from the Caelum tokens (don't theme in parallel)
built components read semantic tokens only — no hardcoded values (token-only invariant)
light/dark/density/RTL are token-layer operations not component changes
primitive vs semantic token tiers
logical properties + CDK bidi for RTL
density as the parity lever (R4)
the no-literal lint as the mechanical gate
books.summary: The token bridge that makes Caelum's two render paths (Angular Material components + CDK/Aria-built components) look like one design system: a single semantic --cae-* CSS-custom-property token set as the source of design truth, with Material 22's --mat-sys-* system tokens bound from it via mat.theme()/mat.theme-overrides(); built components read semantic tokens only (the token-only invariant, no hardcoded design values); light/dark via light-dark() or a theme selector; density as the PrimeNG-parity lever (R4); RTL via logical properties + CDK bidi. Implements D-04. Material 22 theming specifics grounded in research/notes/angular-22-platform.md.

books.title: CDK Primitives
key_concepts: the CDK is headless behavior + a11y with no visual opinion
build on CDK primitives, never bespoke DOM/focus/overlay
overlay + portal is the floating-UI substrate everything floating sits on
the a11y package is the parity engine (key managers + focus trap + live announcer + focus monitor)
cdk-virtual-scroll is the R1 scale lever — grid-vs-table by row count
drag-drop backs OrderList/PickList/FileUpload/Carousel/Splitter
CDK infrastructure (overlay/portal/drag/scroll/a11y) is always-CDK; pattern directives (menu/listbox/stepper/tree) overlap Angular Aria
the four-legged stool: CDK behavior + token design + signal CD + CVA forms
books.summary: The Angular CDK as Caelum's primary build substrate — the headless behavior + accessibility layer Material itself is built on, supplying behavior with no visual opinion. The floating-UI substrate (Overlay + Portal); the a11y package as the parity engine (FocusTrap/FocusMonitor/LiveAnnouncer/InteractivityChecker + the ListKeyManager/FocusKeyManager/ActiveDescendantKeyManager family behind every list-like widget); layout/scrolling incl. cdk-virtual-scroll as the R1 scale lever (grid-vs-table by row count); drag-drop behind OrderList/PickList/FileUpload/Carousel/Splitter; and the structural directives (CdkMenu/CdkListbox/CdkStepper/CdkTree/CdkTable). The opinionated default: every Build-* component composes CDK behavior + reads only token-bridge design (Book 04) + drives CD with signals (Book 01) + bridges forms with a CVA (Book 02) — the four-legged stool. Names where CDK pattern directives overlap Angular Aria (Book 06) and where CDK infrastructure has no Aria replacement. Grounds D-02; CDK 22 package inventory + a11y-utility list grounded in research/notes/angular-22-platform.md.

books.title: Angular Aria Headless Primitives
key_concepts: Angular Aria is 12 first-party headless WAI-ARIA patterns, new and stable in v22
headless: Aria gives keyboard/ARIA/focus/screen-reader, you give DOM+CSS+logic
the reach-for ladder made precise: Material -> Aria -> CDK -> bespoke
Aria stands ON the CDK, it does not replace it (an Aria menu opens via cdkConnectedOverlay)
Aria-vs-CDK is a choice only at the pattern layer, never the infrastructure layer
style headless patterns off ARIA attributes ([aria-selected]) with --cae-* tokens
[formField] inside, ControlValueAccessor outside
prefer Aria but keep the CDK fallback live (maturity caveat); Aria Grid is not a virtualized data grid
books.summary: Angular Aria — Angular's new-in-v22 set of 12 first-party headless WAI-ARIA patterns (Autocomplete/Listbox/Select/Multiselect/Combobox; Menu/Menubar/Toolbar; Accordion/Tabs/Tree/Grid): directives that supply keyboard/ARIA/focus/screen-reader behavior while you supply DOM, CSS, and logic. The book's core is the reach-for ladder made precise — Material (complete-without-styling) -> Aria (standard pattern + custom styling) -> raw CDK (lower-level primitive) -> bespoke — plus the load-bearing clarification that Aria stands ON the CDK (an Aria menu opens through cdkConnectedOverlay), so 'Aria vs CDK' is a choice only at the pattern layer, never the infrastructure layer. Headless patterns style off ARIA attributes with --cae-* tokens (a clean token-bridge fit); value-bearing patterns wear a ControlValueAccessor outside and [formField] inside; and because Aria is the youngest of the three first-party layers, the posture is prefer-Aria-but-keep-the-CDK-fallback-live, gated per-pattern on parity scenarios. Refines D-02 (the Material->Aria->CDK ladder, DEC-ARIA-LADDER). Pattern count + directive-level API grounded in research/notes/angular-22-platform.md.

books.title: Form Control Fundamentals
key_concepts: a control is not the form: value transport (control) vs validity (form) vs presentation (mat-form-field)
every text control implements CVA + MatFormFieldControl together
the four honest states: value/disabled/touched/errorState and their contract members
writeValue never re-emits onChange; null/''/0 are distinct (value fidelity, R3)
the form owns validation policy; a control may only contribute structural validity via NG_VALIDATORS
one library-wide ErrorStateMatcher for uniform error timing (R4)
errorState drives both the error slot and aria-invalid; setDescribedByIds -> aria-describedby
stateChanges must emit on every state change or the form-field won't re-render
FloatLabel/IconField are mat-form-field features; InputGroup is a thin cae-input-group compose (the honest gap)
the forms leg of the four-legged stool (Book 05 §3.6); works under Reactive AND Signal Forms unchanged
books.summary: The control-author's complete contract for a Caelum form field: every text-like cae-* control implements ControlValueAccessor (the forms-API-agnostic value seam, Book 02 §3.4) AND MatFormFieldControl (the presentation seam into mat-form-field, Book 02 §3.6) together. Its spine is the separation of three responsibilities — value transport (the control), validity policy (the form's ValidatorFns), and presentation/error-timing (mat-form-field + one library-wide ErrorStateMatcher) — and the four states a control must render honestly (value/disabled/touched/errorState) with the CVA + MatFormFieldControl member behind each. Covers the CVA contract in full (writeValue never re-emits; null/''/0 distinct; value fidelity R3); validation (the form owns policy, a control may only contribute STRUCTURAL validity via the Validator interface + NG_VALIDATORS — never business rules; cross-field on the group, async via resource()/rxResource()); error state & timing (ErrorStateMatcher default invalid && (touched || submitted), one matcher library-wide for parity R4, errorState -> aria-invalid); the MatFormFieldControl surface (stateChanges discipline, empty/focused/shouldLabelFloat, setDescribedByIds -> aria-describedby); and the PrimeNG FloatLabel/IconField/InputGroup mapping (FloatLabel/IconField are mat-form-field features, InputGroup is the honest gap needing a thin cae-input-group compose). Closes with the forms leg of the four-legged stool (Book 05 §3.6) as a control-authoring checklist. Builds on Book 02 §3.3-§3.6 and Book 06 §3.4; CVA/MatFormFieldControl/ErrorStateMatcher are stable pre-v22 APIs, Signal-Forms validation frontier grounded in research/notes/angular-22-platform.md.

books.title: Numeric, Mask & Specialized Inputs
key_concepts: most of these are format/parse DIRECTIVES on a native matInput, not new components (composition over invention)
the model/view split: model the typed value, display a localized formatted string, parse<->format round-trip via the CVA
use the platform Intl.NumberFormat, never a foreign mask library (provenance Book 03 + fidelity)
the R3 scar: grouping/locale/currency/paste/negatives/caret/IME are where 'looks the same' hides real differences
caret preservation under reformat is the fiddliest behavior; 0 stays distinct from empty/null
filter input on beforeinput, never by blocking keydown (preserves paste/IME/a11y); key-filtering is a UX nicety, not validation
InputOtp is one CVA value across N cells with roving FocusKeyManager focus and paste-spread
password strength meter is advisory UI styled via tokens; the enforced policy is a ValidatorFn the form owns
locale-matrix round-trip testing (en-US/de-DE/RTL + paste + negatives) is the non-negotiable parity gate
books.summary: The directive-heavy parity tier on top of the Book 07 control contract: InputNumber, InputMask, KeyFilter, InputOtp, and the Password strength meter. Core thesis: most of these are format/parse DIRECTIVES on a native matInput (composition over a new control — the directive inherits Material's label/error/a11y), promoted to a thin cae-* component only when the DOM shape itself differs (InputOtp's N segments, Password's meter). The spine is the model/view split (the R3 scar): model the TYPED value (a number, the unmasked digits, the combined code), display a LOCALIZED formatted string, and bridge them with the CVA — writeValue formats in, a keystroke parses out — so the form only ever sees the model. Built on the platform's Intl.NumberFormat rather than a foreign mask library (provenance Book 03 + fidelity). Covers InputNumber (Intl formatting, locale-aware parsing, caret preservation, 0-vs-empty, structural validity via NG_VALIDATORS); InputMask (unmasked model, beforeinput/paste, slots); KeyFilter (filter on beforeinput not keydown, IME-safe, and it is NOT validation); InputOtp (one value across N cells, roving FocusKeyManager focus, paste-spread, autocomplete=one-time-code); and Password+meter (advisory token-styled meter over a real ValidatorFn; scoring lib gated on the Book 03 admit/reject). Closes with the shared discipline: the Book 07 contract + locale-matrix round-trip testing (en-US/de-DE/RTL + paste + negatives + 0/null) as the parity gate. Numeric/mask rests on stable web-platform APIs (Intl.NumberFormat, beforeinput, composition); CDK text-field + version-specific points grounded in research/notes/angular-22-platform.md.

books.title: Overlay & Menu Components
key_concepts: everything floating sits on the CDK overlay substrate (Book 05 §3.1) — never hand-positioned absolute divs
the trust-model split: command overlays return an action/nothing (no CVA), value-bearing overlays are form controls whose option UI floats (full Book 07 contract)
the five-beat overlay lifecycle: open -> position/flip -> focus (trap or rove) -> dismiss -> restore focus to trigger
a shared overlay shell: position presets + scroll/backdrop policy as behavioral DI (provideCaelum), token-only surface
the menu reach-for ladder: MatMenu (Direct) -> Angular Aria menu (net-new, opens via cdkConnectedOverlay) -> CDK Menu (cdkContextMenuTriggerFor / nested) -> bespoke
ConfirmDialog is a build-once CaeConfirmService over MatDialog with role=alertdialog and safe-default focus
value-bearing pickers (CascadeSelect/TreeSelect/Mention) emit the MODEL (leaf/ids/reference), never the display text
the overlay-authoring checklist: dismissal (Escape + outside-click) / focus restoration / positioning-flip / ARIA roles / token-only surface, plus the value boundary for pickers
PanelMenu and Breadcrumb are inline navigation (MatExpansionPanel / semantic nav + aria-current), not overlays
books.summary: The floating-UI tier: OverlayPanel/Popover, ConfirmPopup, ConfirmDialog, the menu family (context/tiered/panel/menubar), Breadcrumb, and the value-bearing pickers CascadeSelect, TreeSelect, and Mention — all built on the CDK overlay substrate (Book 05 §3.1) rather than hand-positioned divs. The book's spine is the trust-model split: a COMMAND overlay (OverlayPanel, ConfirmPopup, ConfirmDialog, any menu, Breadcrumb) shows transient content or triggers an action and returns nothing to a form (no CVA), while a VALUE-BEARING overlay (CascadeSelect, TreeSelect, Mention) is a form control whose option UI happens to float and therefore wears the full Book 07 contract (CVA + MatFormFieldControl + library ErrorStateMatcher), emitting the model (leaf/node-ids/reference) and never the display text. Every component runs the same five-beat lifecycle (open -> position/flip -> focus trap-or-rove -> dismiss on Escape+outside-click -> restore focus to the trigger), enforced once by a shared overlay shell (position presets + scroll/backdrop policy as behavioral DI per Book 01 §3.3; token-only surface per Book 04 §3.6). Menus climb the reach-for ladder (Book 06 §3.1): MatMenu (Direct) for the common case and Menubar, Angular Aria menu for net-new signal-era work (it opens through cdkConnectedOverlay, Book 06 §3.2), CDK Menu (cdkContextMenuTriggerFor / nested cdkMenuTriggerFor, Book 05 §3.5) for ContextMenu/TieredMenu; PanelMenu (MatExpansionPanel, Book 11) and Breadcrumb (semantic nav + aria-current) are inline navigation, not overlays. ConfirmDialog is a build-once CaeConfirmService over MatDialog (role=alertdialog, safe-default focus on Cancel). Closes with the overlay-authoring checklist (dismissal/focus/positioning/roles/token-surface + the value boundary) as this book's parity leg, verified with axe + keyboard + SR (Book 16). The overlay/portal infrastructure is stable pre-cutoff (asserted via Book 05); Aria-menu maturity, the native Popover API + CSS anchor positioning, and inert are watched in §5, grounded in research/notes/angular-22-platform.md.

books.title: Data Tables & Virtualization
key_concepts: a table is a DataSource<T> + column definitions, not a monolithic widget (CdkTable engine, MatTable is the styled skin)
the R1 scar: decide grid-vs-table BY ROW COUNT, per screen — hundreds=MatTable, thousands rendered=MatTable+cdk-virtual-scroll, tens of thousands/server-owned/grouping/export=the grid adapter
the DataSource seam decides client-side (MatTableDataSource, in-memory) vs server-side (fetch one page per sort/page/filter change) — where the data lives is the silent half of R1
MatTable composition: MatSort (+LiveAnnouncer/aria-sort) + MatPaginator + sticky rows/columns + expandable detail rows, all Compose/Direct
virtual scroll OR paginate the same data, never both; fixed row height is the fast default, variable height is the fiddly seam
TreeTable = MatTable over a CdkTree data source (role=treegrid) — the DATA cousin of Book 09 TreeSelect: TreeTable displays the hierarchy, TreeSelect resolves a value from it
the advanced data grid is the genuine gap (Book 13 TanStack behind a neutral adapter); the Aria Grid PATTERN (Book 06) is a keyboard model, NOT a virtualized data grid

the table-authoring checklist: R1 decision recorded + data source matches reality + stable row identity + a11y (role/aria-sort/LiveAnnouncer) + token-only styling + performance profiled at realistic scale
books.summary: The highest-leverage component family for a team leaving PrimeNG (tables top the usage signal). MatTable — a styled skin over the headless CdkTable (Book 05 §3.5) — covers the large majority of real tables by composition: MatSort (with the CDK LiveAnnouncer + aria-sort for screen-reader sort announcements, Book 05 §3.2), MatPaginator, sticky rows/columns, and expandable detail rows (all Compose/Direct, brief §3 data rows). The book's spine is the R1 scar: decide grid-vs-table BY ROW COUNT, per screen, not as a blanket default (brief §8) — hundreds of client-side rows -> MatTable + MatTableDataSource; thousands rendered -> MatTable + cdk-virtual-scroll (the R1 lever, Book 05 §3.3); tens of thousands, server-owned data, grouping, column resize/reorder, virtual rows, or export -> the grid adapter (Book 13, TanStack behind a neutral interface). The DataSource<T> seam (connect/disconnect) is where client-side vs server-side is decided, and where the data LIVES is the silent half — virtualizing the render does nothing if the backend already shipped 50k rows. Covers the client-side vs server-side data source (in-flight/empty/error states, debounce, total count); MatTable composition; virtual scroll + tables (fixed-height happy path, the sticky-header-survival and variable-height seams, virtualize-OR-paginate); TreeTable (MatTable over @angular/cdk/tree, role=treegrid — the DATA cousin of Book 09 TreeSelect's form-control job over the same hierarchy); and when to reach for the grid adapter (the line drawn by R1 + feature set; the adapter is isolated and provenance-vetted per Book 03; the Aria Grid pattern of Book 06 §2.2 is a keyboard model, not a data grid). Closes with the table-authoring checklist (R1 decision recorded, data source matches reality, stable row identity/trackBy, a11y role/aria-sort/LiveAnnouncer, token-only styling per Book 04 §3.6, performance profiled at realistic scale per Book 18 with OnPush+signal CD per Book 01 §3.2). MatTable/MatSort/MatPaginator/cdk-virtual-scroll are stable pre-cutoff; signal data sources + zoneless table perf + virtual-scroll autosize grounded in research/notes/angular-22-platform.md.

books.title: Layout, Panels, Media & Drag-Drop
key_concepts: most layout/panel components are Direct Material ports token-skinned for density/parity (accordion/card/tabs/stepper/toolbar/divider) — don't rebuild what Material ships (the laziness ladder lived)
one engine, a column of components: @angular/cdk/drag-drop (Book 05 §3.4) backs OrderList/PickList/FileUpload/Carousel/Splitter — built once on the CDK, never a foreign drag library (Book 03)
the two honest gaps with no first-party equivalent: Splitter (Build-M, CDK drag divider + flex, keyboard-resizable separator) and ScrollPanel (Build-S, usually native overflow + CDK scrolling)
media is a team priority: Carousel (CDK drag + stepper index, autoplay pause-on-focus), Galleria/Image-preview (lightbox on the Book 09 §3.1 overlay shell), large galleries virtualize (Book 10 §3.3)

the drag-drop parity leg: a keyboard path for every drag (no mouse-only reorder), LiveAnnouncer for moves, RTL axes via Directionality, drag state in signals under zoneless (Book 01 §3.2), drop validation + virtualization
FileUpload validates type/size at the trust boundary before upload and may wear a CVA (Book 07); no first-party uploader so build it US-clean
the build-vs-vet fork for a heavy Carousel runs through the Book 03 admit/reject gate
this book COMPLETES Volume II
books.summary: The book that completes Volume II: the structural/visual remainder — accordion, card, tabs, stepper, toolbar, divider (overwhelmingly Direct Material ports), the honest gaps Splitter and ScrollPanel, the drag-drop cluster OrderList/PickList/FileUpload, and the media family Carousel/Galleria/Image-preview (a team priority). Core thesis: this is the laziness ladder lived — most layout/panel widgets are Direct (MatExpansionPanel/MatCard/MatTabGroup/MatStepper/MatToolbar/MatDivider) and Caelum's job is to token-skin them for PrimeNG density/parity (Book 04 §3.6) and verify the a11y Material already ships, NOT rebuild them; the 'Misc' tail (pDraggable->CDK DragDrop, FocusTrap->cdkTrapFocus, Ripple->matRipple) is the same one-line-reach lesson. The genuine builds cluster around ONE engine — @angular/cdk/drag-drop (Book 05 §3.4) backs OrderList (reorder within), PickList (transfer between two connected drop lists), FileUpload (drop target + HttpClient, validate type/size at the trust boundary, no first-party uploader so build US-clean), Carousel (drag-to-advance over a stepper index), and Splitter (draggable, keyboard-resizable separator) — built once on the CDK, never a foreign drag library (Book 03). The media family layers the overlay shell (Book 09 §3.1) for Galleria/Image-preview lightboxes on top of drag-drop, all token-styled, with large galleries virtualizing their thumbnail strip (Book 10 §3.3); the Carousel's 'or vetted US lib if heavy' is a live build-vs-vet fork through the Book 03 gate. The cluster's parity leg (the most-skipped requirement): a keyboard path for EVERY drag (drag-only fails accessibility + parity), LiveAnnouncer move announcements (Book 05 §3.2), RTL drag axes via Directionality, drag state in signals under a zoneless host (Book 01 §3.2 — the one safe NgZone.runOutsideAngular), drop validation, and virtualization. ScrollPanel stays as lazy as native overflow + token-styled scrollbars + CDK scrolling (Book 05 §3.3) allows. 'Done' splits by tier: Direct ports = parity-verify a token skin; gaps/cluster = an honest CDK build with the keyboard path included from the start (verified axe + keyboard + SR, Book 16). Completes Volume II — next is Volume III's adapter layer (Books 12-15). Material panels/CDK drag-drop are stable pre-cutoff; CSS scroll-snap/container-queries/native-dialog futures grounded in research/notes/angular-22-platform.md.

books.title: The Adapter Pattern
key_concepts: the adapter is the bottom rung of the reach-for ladder — below bespoke (Material->Aria->CDK->bespoke->adapter)
you only reach for it for an engine-sized gap, after the ladder is exhausted AND the lib clears Book 03's admit/reject gate
design the neutral app-owned interface FIRST, in cae-* vocabulary, then make the library satisfy it (inversion / ports-and-adapters)
never leak a vendor type across the interface — the neutrality test is 'satisfiable two ways' (library AND fallback)
only the adapter file imports the 3p lib — one membrane, vendor types inside, Caelum types outside
isolation pays off three ways: provenance is one file (R5), swap is one file not N call sites, honest dependency accounting
adapter erosion (R6) is enforced MECHANICALLY with ESLint no-restricted-imports scoping the package to its adapter file (CI-failing, self-explaining, one allow-listed path)
inject the adapter via an InjectionToken + provideCaelumX(); swap = change one provider (Book 01 §3.3)
theme the foreign render path through --cae-* tokens — headless/render-it-yourself libs keep the render inside the token bridge (Book 04 §3.6)
every adapter is provisional by design — it retires when a first-party primitive fills the gap
the indirection has a real cost; for a small need the right answer is Book 03's don't-add-the-dependency
books.summary: The opener of Volume III (the adapter layer): the single discipline that makes depending on a foreign library safe, applied identically to the three genuine gaps (data grid, charts, editor — Books 13-15). The adapter is the BOTTOM RUNG of the reach-for ladder (Material->Aria->CDK->bespoke->adapter, Book 01 §3.4 / Book 06 §3.1) — reached only when a gap is not a missing widget but a missing ENGINE that an existing battle-tested MIT US-origin library solves better than a bespoke CDK build would, and only after the dependency clears Book 03's admit/reject gate (the cheapest dependency is still the one you don't add). The method is interface-first: design a NEUTRAL, APP-OWNED interface in Caelum's vocabulary (cae-* components, signal IO, --cae-* tokens, domain-typed values) and make the library satisfy IT — the ports-and-adapters/hexagonal inversion (and GoF Adapter); never leak a vendor type across the boundary (the neutrality test: satisfiable two ways, by the library AND by the documented fallback — MatTable / contenteditable, brief §4). A SINGLE adapter file is the only code in the whole library that imports the foreign package — a membrane with vendor types inside and Caelum types outside. That one rule ('only the adapter touches it') pays off three ways (brief §2.1): provenance is one file plus the lib's transitive tree (R5; one npm ls root + license scan, Book 03 §3.2), a failed-revet/acquired library is swapped at one file not N call sites, and dependency accounting stays honest. It is fragile — it dies the first 'just this once' direct import (adapter erosion, R6) — so it is enforced MECHANICALLY with ESLint no-restricted-imports scoping the package to its adapter file (CI-failing, self-explaining error, one allow-listed path), backed by the review lens and the provenance CI gate (Book 03 §3.4). The interface is wired through DI as an InjectionToken provided by provideCaelumX() (the provide*() shape of Book 09 §3.1; behavior-by-injection, Book 01 §3.3), so 'swap the library' = 'change one provider' and the fallback is a first-class selectable implementation. The foreign render path is themed through --cae-* tokens (Book 04 §3.6) — Caelum's three candidates are all headless/render-it-yourself precisely so the render stays inside the token bridge (a lib shipping opinionated CSS is a worse candidate). Closes with the adapter-authoring checklist (neutral/single-membrane/DI-swappable/token-themed/provenance-signed-off/a11y-parity) as this book's discipline leg, the bottom-rung analogue of Book 05 §3.6. Implements D-03; the three concrete adapters are TanStack/D-07 (Book 13), visx/D-08 deferred (Book 14), Lexical/D-09 (Book 15), with final transitive-provenance sign-off at M2. The adapter pattern is stable architecture (GoF Adapter; Cockburn's hexagonal/ports-and-adapters); the Angular-22 DI/signals/zoneless specifics it leans on are grounded in Books 01-04 and research/notes/angular-22-platform.md.

books.title: Data Grid Adapter
key_concepts: the advanced data grid is the first genuine gap (Book 10 §3.5) — server-side everything, grouping, column resize/reorder, virtual rows at scale, export — an ENGINE not a widget
Book 12's pattern instantiated unchanged: neutral CaeGridAdapter interface, the single grid.adapter.ts membrane, ESLint no-restricted-imports, DI swap via CAE_GRID + provideCaelumGrid()
TanStack Table is HEADLESS — it renders no DOM, so Caelum draws the cells itself with --cae-* Material markup and there is no vendor stylesheet to fight (headless is the right adapter shape)
the neutral interface is the swap insurance — and the grid is the highest provenance-risk dependency, so swappability matters most here (satisfiable two ways: TanStack AND the MatTable fallback)
server-side everything reuses Book 10 §3.1's DataSource seam — sort/page/group become CaeGridDataRequest emissions, never a full client load
grouping/expansion/TreeTable are modes of one engine behind neutral state (role=treegrid through the neutral surface)

the MatTable + cdk-virtual-scroll fallback (MatGridAdapter) satisfies the same CAE_GRID token — a provenance escape hatch and the neutrality proof; it is a floor, not full column-virtualization parity
thin-vs-thick: depend on @tanstack/table-core directly (zero-dep, render in Caelum's template) vs the @tanstack/angular-table wrapper + FlexRender — Caelum defaults to core-direct
all TanStack version/API/provenance facts route through research/notes/tanstack-table.md (frontier, staling), NEVER asserted from memory or cited as a Book §
implements D-07; final transitive-provenance + origin sign-off defers to M2 (the tree is clean: MIT table-core zero-dep + 0BSD tslib, both US-maintained)
books.summary: The first CONCRETE adapter — Book 12's pattern applied, unchanged, to the advanced data grid, the gap Book 10 §3.5 drew the line at (server-side everything, grouping/aggregation, column resize/reorder, virtual rows at scale, export — an ENGINE, not a widget, so it sits on the ladder's bottom rung). The engine is TanStack Table, chosen because it is HEADLESS: it renders no DOM (it supplies row models + state + API), so the cae-data-grid component draws every cell itself with --cae-* Material markup (Book 04 §3.6) and there is no vendor stylesheet to fight — headless is the ideal adapter shape (a batteries-included grid shipping its own CSS would be worse). The structure is Book 12's, instantiated: a NEUTRAL CaeGridAdapter interface in Caelum's vocabulary (CaeColumn<T>/CaeSort/CaeRowGroup/CaeGridDataRequest, no vendor ColumnDef leaks — the satisfiable-two-ways neutrality test, Book 12 §3.1); a SINGLE grid.adapter.ts that is the only file importing @tanstack/* (mapping CaeColumn->ColumnDef, composing the opt-in row models getCoreRowModel/getSortedRowModel/getGroupedRowModel/getExpandedRowModel, reading rows back into a viewRows signal), fenced by the ESLint no-restricted-imports rule against adapter erosion (Book 12 §3.2-§3.3, R6); DI swap via CAE_GRID + provideCaelumGrid() (Book 12 §3.4; the provide*() precedent Book 09 §3.1); and a MatTable + cdk-virtual-scroll FALLBACK (MatGridAdapter) satisfying the same token (brief §4; Book 10 §3.1/§3.3) — the provenance escape hatch and the neutrality proof, honest that it is a floor, not full column-virtualization parity. Covers the vendor-free interface design; the single membrane; rendering cells through the token bridge with cdk-virtual-scroll for virtual rows (the R1 lever, Book 10 §3.3) and the thin-vs-thick fork (own the render vs adopt FlexRender — Caelum defaults to depending on the zero-dep @tanstack/table-core directly, Book 12 §5); server-side everything via the DataSource seam (Book 10 §3.1) as CaeGridDataRequest/applyServerResult, never a full client load; grouping/expansion/TreeTable (Book 10 §3.4) as modes of one engine behind neutral state (role=treegrid through the neutral surface); and DI wiring + the fallback + export (a neutral CaeGridAdapter capability, not a second vendor lock-in). Closes with the seven-leg grid-adapter checklist (neutral/single-membrane+ESLint/DI-swappable+fallback/token-themed/server-side-seam/a11y-through-the-neutral-surface/provenance-signed-at-M2), the concrete analogue of Book 12 §3.6 and Book 10 §3.6. The a11y model uses role=grid/treegrid + aria-sort + LiveAnnouncer (Book 05 §3.2); the Aria Grid PATTERN (Book 06 §2.2) supplies the keyboard model, NOT the data engine (Book 10 §3.5's boundary). Implements D-07; all TanStack version/API/provenance specifics are grounded in research/notes/tanstack-table.md (frontier — headless, MIT table-core 8.21.3 zero-dep + angular-table 8.21.4, the clean two-package permissive US-origin tree with tslib 0BSD/Microsoft), cited as a research note never as a Book §, with the final transitive-provenance + origin sign-off deferring to M2 (Book 03 §3.1).

books.title: Charts Adapter
key_concepts: charts are the gap Material fills NOT AT ALL — a pure third-party case from zero

D-08 endorsed visx but visx is REACT-BOUND (hard react peer on @visx/shape) — structurally unavailable to an Angular library; the Angular render-it-yourself path uses D3's framework-agnostic modules directly (d3-scale/d3-shape, ISC, Mike Bostock/US — visx's own substrate). A refinement of D-08, not a reversal — filed DEC-CHARTS-LIB for M2
the neutral interface is what made the visx->D3 pivot a one-file change, not a rewrite — the neutrality test (satisfiable two ways: D3 AND hand-rolled SVG) earning its keep before a chart is drawn
render-it-yourself = D3 emits only numbers + SVG path strings (no DOM, no CSS), so cae-chart draws the SVG itself with --cae-* tokens (the ideal token-bridge fit, like the headless grid)
Book 12's structure instantiated: neutral CaeChartAdapter, the single chart.adapter.ts membrane over d3-*, ESLint no-restricted-imports, DI swap via CAE_CHART + provideCaelumChart()

a11y is the HARD case: a chart is not just a picture — accessible name + text summary + a data-table alternative + keyboard for interactive points, all carried by the component (D3 emits geometry not semantics)

'size the need' (brief §9.3): charts are DEFERRED and the d3 tree is bushier (d3-scale pulls 5 sub-modules), so the no-dependency hand-rolled-SVG fallback is often the PREFERRED impl for small needs — DI makes it a selectable provider
SVG is the default render target (token-themable, accessible, serializable for SSR); canvas/WebGL for very large series is a future mode that loses theming+a11y unless mirrored
implements D-08 (DEFERRED for the build — team not using charts yet — but written in volume order); the library refinement + the d3-module sign-off at M2
all visx/d3 version/library/provenance facts route through research/notes/visx-charts.md, NEVER memory or a Book §
books.summary: The second CONCRETE adapter — Book 12's pattern (and Book 13's grid template) applied to charts, the one gap Angular Material fills NOT AT ALL (a pure third-party case from zero). The decisive research finding (research/notes/visx-charts.md): D-08 endorsed visx, but visx is REACT-BOUND (a hard react ^18||^19 peer on @visx/shape) and so structurally unavailable to an Angular library — the visx APPROACH (render-it-yourself viz primitives over D3) is right, but the Angular instantiation depends on D3's framework-agnostic modules DIRECTLY (d3-scale 4.0.2 + d3-shape 3.2.0, ISC, Mike Bostock/US — visx's own substrate). This is a REFINEMENT of D-08, not a reversal, filed as DEC-CHARTS-LIB for the M2 sign-off; and it cost almost nothing precisely because the neutral interface never named visx, so discovering visx won't work is a one-file adapter choice, not a rewrite (the neutrality test earning its keep). Render-it-yourself is the ideal token-bridge fit: D3 emits only numbers + SVG path strings (no DOM, no CSS), so cae-chart draws the <path>/<rect>/<text> itself with --cae-* tokens and a --cae-chart-series-* color ramp (Book 04 §3.6; Book 13 §3.3). Structure is Book 12's: a neutral CaeChartAdapter interface (Caelum-typed series/marks/axes, no d3 type leaks — Book 12 §3.1), a single chart.adapter.ts membrane importing d3-* fenced by ESLint no-restricted-imports (Book 12 §3.2-§3.3, R6), DI swap via CAE_CHART + provideCaelumChart() (Book 12 §3.4; Book 09 §3.1). Two things bite harder than for the grid: A11Y — a chart is NOT just a picture, so the component carries an accessible name + text summary + a visually-hidden data-table alternative + keyboard for interactive points (D3 emits geometry not semantics; Book 13 §3.6 leg 6, Book 16); and 'SIZE THE NEED' (brief §9.3) — because charts are deferred and the d3 tree is bushier (d3-scale pulls 5 sub-modules), the zero-dependency hand-rolled-SVG fallback (a sparkline is one <polyline>) is often the PREFERRED implementation for small needs, made a selectable provider by DI (Book 03's 'cheapest dependency is the one you don't add', Book 12 §6). Covers the vendor-free interface; the single membrane over d3-*; drawing token-themed SVG; the chart a11y case; DI + the no-dep fallback as the size-the-need lever; and the seven-leg chart checklist (neutral/single-membrane+ESLint/DI-swappable+no-dep-fallback/token-themed-SVG/a11y-carried-by-component/provenance-at-M2/need-sized). Bleeding edge: the library is genuinely undecided (don't pin before M2 — the interface is the insurance; every adapter is provisional, Book 12 §5); the d3 tree vs the lazier path (a profiled bundle decision, Book 18); zoneless + large-data downsampling + SVG-serializable SSR (canvas/WebGL a future mode that loses theming+a11y). Implements D-08 — DEFERRED for the build (team not using charts yet — ROADMAP M3/cut order) but written in volume order; all visx/d3 facts grounded in research/notes/visx-charts.md (frontier, cited as a research note never as a Book §), with the library refinement + the d3-module sign-off deferring to M2 (Book 03 §3.1).

books.title: Rich-Text Editor Adapter
key_concepts: the rich-text editor is the third genuine gap (Material ships no editor at all) and the adapter that COMPLETES Volume III

the FAVORABLE MIRROR of the visx finding: core `lexical` is a framework-agnostic, dependency-free editor engine with NO react dep or peer (sole dep @lexical/internal, a zero-dep MIT leaf), and the headless behavior modules @lexical/rich-text/@lexical/history/@lexical/utils are React-free too; the React peer is quarantined entirely in the separately-published @lexical/react binding Angular never imports — so D-09 STANDS AS-IS, no refinement (unlike D-08's visx->D3 pivot)

the neutral interface made the framework-binding question cost nothing EITHER way: React-bound core -> contenteditable fallback; React-free core -> bind Lexical; same cae-editor above the membrane (the neutrality test, satisfiable two ways: Lexical AND the contenteditable fallback)

Book 12's structure instantiated: neutral CaeEditorAdapter, the single editor.adapter.ts membrane over lexical/@lexical/*, ESLint no-restricted-imports + the EDITOR-SPECIFIC clause that @lexical/react stays out of package.json entirely (so the React peer can't leak), DI swap via CAE_EDITOR + provideCaelumEditor()
the value crosses the boundary as a NEUTRAL CaeDoc (opaque/serializable), NEVER Lexical's EditorState — the form layer, validators, and persisted data never touch a vendor type

the dimension NEW to this book vs grid/charts: an editor IS a form control — cae-editor implements ControlValueAccessor + mat-form-field integration + the four honest states, exchanging the neutral CaeDoc (Book 07 §3.1/§3.4; Book 06 §3.4)

a11y in two places, both component-owned: the contenteditable surface (accessible name + LiveAnnouncer, Book 05 §3.2) and a roving-tabindex toolbar (aria-pressed reflecting active marks — Aria toolbar pattern Book 06 §2.2/§3.1, CDK FocusKeyManager fallback)

the vanilla/framework-agnostic API: createEditor()/setRootElement() onto a cae-editor-owned contenteditable + registerRichText/registerHistory/createEmptyHistoryState/mergeRegister + editor.update()/registerUpdateListener, all inside the one membrane (research note)

'size the need': the editor is ROADMAP cut order #1 (heaviest gap, narrowest usage), so the no-engine PlainContentEditableAdapter (contenteditable + CDK toolbar + thin command layer) is often the PREFERRED first ship, a selectable provider not a degraded mode (Book 12 §6; brief §4; Book 14 §3.5)

the @lexical/* tree is the cleanest of the three gaps (single-origin Meta, all MIT, over a zero-dep leaf, typescript the only optional peer); the one new external library is collaboration via @lexical/yjs over Yjs, to vet at M2
implements D-09 (DEFERRED for the build — cut order #1 — but written in volume order, COMPLETING Volume III); final sign-off at M2

all Lexical version/library/provenance facts route through research/notes/lexical-editor.md, NEVER memory or a Book § (recall is wrong in BOTH directions — '@lexical/react is famous' tempts the false 'Lexical is React')
books.summary: The third CONCRETE adapter and the one that COMPLETES Volume III — Book 12's pattern (and the Book 13/14 templates) applied to the rich-text editor, the third gap Angular Material fills NOT AT ALL (a pure third-party case from zero). The decisive research finding (research/notes/lexical-editor.md) is the FAVORABLE MIRROR of the visx result: where visx's render core was React-bound (Book 14 §2.3), Lexical's core engine carries NO React dependency at all — core `lexical` is a 'dependency-free text editor engine' (sole dep @lexical/internal, a zero-dep MIT leaf) that works 'independently of any framework or library', and the headless behavior modules (@lexical/rich-text/@lexical/history/@lexical/utils) are React-free too; React is quarantined entirely in the separately-published @lexical/react binding the Angular adapter never imports. So unlike D-08's charts, D-09 STANDS AS-IS — no refinement — and Lexical is consumable in Angular as a REAL adapter, not a fallback-only outcome. The load-bearing lesson (from the opposite direction to Book 14): the neutral interface made the framework-binding question cost nothing EITHER way, since cae-editor binds whichever engine satisfies CaeEditorAdapter (the neutrality test, satisfiable two ways: Lexical AND a hand-rolled contenteditable). Structure is Book 12's: a neutral CaeEditorAdapter interface whose VALUE is a Caelum CaeDoc (opaque/serializable, NOT EditorState — no vendor type leaks, Book 12 §3.1); a single editor.adapter.ts membrane that is the only file importing lexical/@lexical/* (createEditor/setRootElement onto the component's contenteditable + mergeRegister(registerRichText, registerHistory), bridging registerUpdateListener -> a value signal as CaeDoc), fenced by ESLint no-restricted-imports PLUS the editor-specific clause that @lexical/react stays out of package.json entirely so the React peer can't leak (Book 12 §3.2-§3.3, R6); DI swap via CAE_EDITOR + provideCaelumEditor() (Book 12 §3.4; Book 09 §3.1). Two dimensions are NEW to this book. FORM CONTROL: an editor is a form field, so cae-editor implements ControlValueAccessor + mat-form-field integration + the four honest states (value/disabled/touched/error), exchanging the neutral CaeDoc (Book 07 §3.1/§3.4; Book 06 §3.4) — the heaviest instance of the Book 07 control contract. A11Y in two places the component owns: the contenteditable surface (accessible name + LiveAnnouncer, Book 05 §3.2) and a roving-tabindex toolbar (arrow-key nav + aria-pressed active marks — prefer the Aria toolbar pattern Book 06 §2.2/§3.1, fall back to CDK FocusKeyManager). The contenteditable is themed end-to-end through --cae-* tokens (Lexical's theme maps node types to Caelum class names; no vendor stylesheet — Book 04 §3.6; Book 12 §3.5). Covers the vendor-free interface with a neutral value; the single membrane over lexical; rendering the contenteditable through the token bridge; the editor-as-form-control; a11y + the toolbar; DI + the PlainContentEditableAdapter fallback as the size-the-need lever; and the eight-leg editor checklist (neutral+CaeDoc / single-membrane+ESLint+no-@lexical/react / form-control / token-themed / a11y / DI-swappable+fallback / provenance-at-M2 / need-sized). Bleeding edge: Lexical is pre-1.0 and moves fast (don't pin before M2 — the interface is the insurance; every adapter provisional, Book 12 §5); the contenteditable fallback is a genuine FLOOR (cut order #1 — likely the first thing shipped); collaboration via @lexical/yjs over Yjs is a new external library to vet at M2; zoneless needs care (event-driven engine mutating contenteditable outside Angular — bridge listeners to signals, Book 01 §3.2), and a @lexical/headless no-DOM mode exists for SSR/validation. Implements D-09 — DEFERRED for the build (heaviest gap, narrowest usage — ROADMAP cut order #1) but written in volume order, COMPLETING Volume III; all Lexical facts grounded in research/notes/lexical-editor.md (frontier, cited as a research note never as a Book §), with the final sign-off deferring to M2 (Book 03 §3.1).

books.title: Accessibility & Parity Verification
key_concepts: this book OPENS Volume IV (Quality) and is the ENGINE that verifies every component/adapter book's parity leg (Books 09 §3.6, 10 §3.6, 11 §3.5, 13 §3.6, 14 §3.4, 15 §3.4/§3.5)
'looks the same' is NOT 'is the same' — visual parity is the dangerous moment because keyboard/SR/edge-cases stay unverified (brief §6); presume NOT-at-parity until a procedure proves it

the CAPABILITY LEDGER tracks each component through earned states untouched->scaffolded->implementer-passed->adversarial-passed; nothing ships below adversarial-passed (ROADMAP M4); a code change RESETS the state (re-vet discipline, Book 03 §3.4)
PRE-COMMITTED parity scenarios: write the functional/a11y/visual bar BEFORE 'done' can be claimed, derived from the p-* behavior, so the bar can't move to meet the implementation

the IMPLEMENTER-vs-ADVERSARIAL split (brief §7): the builder runs the implementer pass; a SEPARATE party mandated to falsify runs the adversarial pass — the same two-party discipline as adversarial_review; the same agent doing both inherits the implementer's blind spot

three verification LAYERS of decreasing automation/increasing value: axe-core (broad+shallow, necessary-not-sufficient, a merge-gate floor not proof), keyboard operability via the CDK ComponentHarness (focus order, roving keys, dismissal+focus restoration, no unintended trap), screen-reader semantics + the manual adversarial pass (meaningful names, announcements — what no engine catches)
three parity DIMENSIONS the layers serve: functional, accessible, visual (light/dark/RTL + density-as-parity-lever R4 + token-only, Book 04 §3.4/§3.6)

the PROVENANCE lesson: axe-core is MPL-2.0 (weak/file-level COPYLEFT, flagged for a runtime dep) but admissible as a DEV/TEST tool never shipped — Book 03 §3.3's runtime-vs-dev distinction made concrete; grounded in research/notes/a11y-testing-tooling.md
backs the verify_parity skill and definition_of_done's a11y-parity gate (axe+keyboard, adversarial pass for risky/milestone work); the ledger is the M4 milestone exit
visual-regression is a DIMENSION here but a BUILD in Book 17; SR automation is the immature frontier layer (keep Layer 3 manual+ledgered, don't pretend-automate); the test-RUNNER choice is Book 17's
all tool facts (axe-core 4.12.1 MPL-2.0/Deque, @axe-core/playwright, the CDK harness) route through research/notes/a11y-testing-tooling.md, NEVER memory or a Book §
books.summary: The opener of Volume IV (Quality) and the verification ENGINE for the whole library: Volumes I-III built the components and adapters, each ending with a parity leg (Book 09 §3.6, 10 §3.6, 11 §3.5/§3.6, 13 §3.6, 14 §3.4, 15 §3.4/§3.5); this book turns the project's standing promise — 'looks done' is not 'is done' — into a repeatable procedure. Spine (brief §6/§7): (1) 'looks the same' is NOT 'is the same' — a Material component can render pixel-close to its p-* predecessor and still fail a keyboard user, a screen reader, or an edge case, so a component is presumed NOT at parity until a procedure proves it and 'it looks right' is evidence of nothing; (2) the CAPABILITY LEDGER tracks every component through earned states (untouched -> scaffolded -> implementer-passed -> adversarial-passed), nothing ships below adversarial-passed (ROADMAP M4), and a code change resets the state (the Book 03 §3.4 re-vet discipline applied to a11y); (3) PRE-COMMITTED parity scenarios — the functional/a11y/visual checks are authored before 'done' and derived from the p-* behavior (the COMPARISON migration map), so the bar can't silently lower to meet the implementation; (4) the IMPLEMENTER-vs-ADVERSARIAL split — the builder does the implementer pass, a SEPARATE party mandated to falsify does the adversarial pass (the adversarial_review two-party discipline; the same agent doing both collapses into the implementer's blind spot). Verification runs in three LAYERS: axe-core (the Deque engine via @axe-core/playwright — broad+shallow, catches contrast/names/ARIA/ids in ms, necessary-but-not-sufficient, run in BOTH themes + parity density as a 0-violation merge gate, never the proof); keyboard operability via the Angular CDK component test harness (TestbedHarnessEnvironment + documentRootLoader for overlays — drives Tab/arrows/Enter/Escape and asserts what happened: focus order, roving keys via Book 05 §3.2 key managers, dismissal + focus restoration Book 09 §3.6, no unintended trap); and screen-reader semantics + the manual adversarial pass (meaningful names, announced state changes via LiveAnnouncer Book 05 §3.2, the chart-data/editor-toolbar cases — what no engine catches, the most-skipped layer, made a named ledgered step). These serve three parity DIMENSIONS — functional, accessible, visual (light/dark/RTL + density R4 + token-only no-literal, Book 04 §3.4/§3.6) — and a component is green only on all three under both parties. Carries a load-bearing PROVENANCE lesson: axe-core is MPL-2.0 (a weak/file-level COPYLEFT license Book 03 §2.2 would flag for a runtime dep) yet admitted because it is a DEV/TEST tool never shipped in the published package, so the copyleft never reaches Caelum's source (Book 03 §3.3's runtime-vs-dev distinction made concrete — the canonical 'rejected for runtime, fine for CI' example). Closes with the seven-leg verification checklist (scenarios-pre-committed / axe-green-both-themes / keyboard-via-harness / SR-exercised / all-three-dimensions / two-parties / ledger-updated); definition_of_done enforces legs 2-4+6 as the a11y-parity gate, the ledger (leg 7) is the M4 exit. Bleeding edge: automation's hard ceiling (axe catches a minority; SR automation is the immature frontier — keep Layer 3 manual+ledgered, don't let a green axe dashboard masquerade as accessibility); WCAG 2.2 AA is the target but axe's exact tag coverage is verify-against-docs; visual-regression is a DIMENSION here and a BUILD in Book 17 (keep the boundary clean). Backs the verify_parity skill; methodology grounded in brief §6/§7, all tool facts (axe-core 4.12.1 MPL-2.0/Deque/US zero-dep, @axe-core/playwright 4.12.1 peer playwright-core, the CDK harness current in v22 docs) grounded in research/notes/a11y-testing-tooling.md (frontier, cited as a research note never as a Book §), with the dev-tier provenance sign-off at M4/CI. No component is at adversarial-passed yet (no M0 component code); this defines the verification the M0-M4 components run.

books.title: Testing Strategy & Tooling
key_concepts: the test pyramid is BENT for a component library (a 'trophy') — a fat MIDDLE of component tests, because most defects are INTERACTION defects only visible when the component is mounted and driven (Book 16 §2.1's 'looks done' trap in test form)

a test is a pre-committed parity scenario made EXECUTABLE (Book 16 §3.1) — Book 16 decides WHAT to verify + which parity dimension; Book 17 decides WHICH LAYER expresses each clause (validator->unit, keyboard->harness, light/dark/density->snapshot); keep the boundary clean

the FRONTIER fact: Angular 22's DEFAULT unit-test runner is now VITEST (via @angular/build:unit-test, tsconfig.spec.json, ::development); Karma is a SUPPORTED-but-LEGACY alternative (not removed); greenfield Caelum is BORN on Vitest (stable path) and never walks the EXPERIMENTAL Karma-migration path — all runner facts route through research/notes/angular-22-testing.md, never memory or a Book §

default env is Node + jsdom (fast, NO real layout — getBoundingClientRect is 0); Vitest Browser Mode (Playwright/WebdriverIO provider, opt-in) is the escape hatch for layout/focus/zoneless cases jsdom can't serve

the CDK ComponentHarness is the HIGHEST-LEVERAGE artifact + the concrete build behind Book 16 §3.3: a CaeXHarness per component drives semantic ops (not DOM coordinates, survives refactors), IS the executable scenario, is RUNNER-AGNOSTIC + portable (jsdom TestbedHarnessEnvironment AND e2e WebDriver), and makes zoneless settling explicit via whenStable/forceStabilize (Book 01 §3.2)

four test LAYERS: unit (pure logic — signals/format-parse R3 matrix Book 08/validators Book 07 §3.2, TestBed only when DI needed) -> component-via-harness (the bulk) -> integration (a Forge screen, adapter FALLBACK provider swapped in via DI for determinism + neutrality proof, Book 12 §3.2) -> visual/e2e (Playwright real browser)

VISUAL REGRESSION is committed as a DIMENSION in Book 16 §3.5 but BUILT here: Playwright snapshots across light/dark/RTL/density (Book 04 §3.3/§3.4/§3.6) under GOLDEN DISCIPLINE (commit+review baselines like code, never auto-accept in CI; kill flake at the source — disable animations, pin viewport/fonts, mask timestamps, small tolerance; prefer a harness assertion to a snapshot for semantic props); @axe-core/playwright rides the SAME browser run for real-layout a11y (Book 16 §3.2)

the WHOLE test toolchain (Vitest, jsdom, Playwright, @axe-core/playwright, axe-core) is DEV-TIER provenance — in devDependencies, EXCLUDED from the shipped-tree scan (Book 03 §3.3/§3.4; the M0-4 gate implements the runtime-vs-dev split, axe MPL the worked example); only RUNTIME deps face the full US-origin bar

determinism is non-negotiable (no Date.now/Math.random/live network — inject clock, seed/stub, stub HttpClient); CI: unit+component = fast per-PR gate, integration+visual/e2e = heavier matrix (on-label/milestone/post-merge); golden updates gated by review

backs the add_test skill + definition_of_done's build/test gate; honest status: NO test infra yet (M0 not started), runner choice provisional-pending-M0 (verify Vitest-as-default against the live v22 workspace at scaffold — CONV-1/M0-1)
books.summary: The second book of Volume IV — the TOOLING that executes Book 16's verification and the tests that reach past a11y. Boundary kept clean: Book 16 owns WHAT must be verified and why (the ledger, pre-committed scenarios, the three a11y layers); Book 17 owns the RUNNER, the harness depth, the visual-regression BUILD, and CI wiring (visual-regression is a DIMENSION in Book 16 §3.5, a BUILD here). Spine: (1) the test pyramid is BENT for a component library into a 'trophy' — a fat MIDDLE of component tests, because Caelum's value is behavior at the DOM and most defects are INTERACTION defects only visible when a component is mounted and driven (Book 16 §2.1's 'looks done' trap, in test form); a library over-investing in unit tests ships a green suite and a broken keyboard path. (2) A test is a pre-committed parity scenario (Book 16 §3.1) made EXECUTABLE — Book 16 decides what + which of the three parity dimensions; Book 17 decides which LAYER expresses each clause (validator->unit, keyboard->harness, light/dark/density->snapshot); the capability-ledger states (Book 16 §2.2) are EARNED by these tests passing, and a code change resets them (Book 03 §3.4 re-vet). (3) The FRONTIER fact: Angular 22's DEFAULT unit-test runner is now VITEST (via the @angular/build:unit-test builder — tsConfig tsconfig.spec.json, buildTarget ::development), with Karma a SUPPORTED-but-LEGACY alternative (not removed — correcting 'Karma is dead'); the STABLE path is greenfield, so Caelum is BORN on Vitest and never walks the EXPERIMENTAL Karma-migration path (the refactor-jasmine-vitest schematic is experimental/partial). Default env is Node + jsdom (fast, NO real layout); Vitest Browser Mode (Playwright provider, opt-in) is the escape hatch for layout/focus/zoneless cases. All runner facts route through research/notes/angular-22-testing.md, never memory or a Book §. FOUR LAYERS: unit (pure logic without a fixture — signal/computed derivations Book 02, the R3 format/parse locale matrix Book 08, validators Book 07 §3.2; TestBed only when DI is required); component-via-harness (THE BULK + the concrete build behind Book 16 §3.3 — a custom CaeXHarness per component exposing semantic ops on locatorFor/HarnessPredicate, loaded via TestbedHarnessEnvironment with documentRootLoader for overlays Book 09 §3.6, testing semantic interaction not coordinates so it survives refactors, IS the executable scenario, runner-agnostic + portable across jsdom and e2e, and awaiting whenStable/forceStabilize for zoneless settling Book 01 §3.2; also asserts the four CVA states Book 07 §2.2/§3.1); integration (a composed Forge screen, with the adapter FALLBACK provider swapped in via provideCaelumGrid for determinism AND to prove the neutrality contract, Book 12 §3.2/§3.4); and visual/e2e (Playwright). VISUAL REGRESSION + GOLDEN DISCIPLINE: Playwright snapshots across light/dark/RTL/parity-density (Book 04 §3.3/§3.4/§3.6) with baselines committed+reviewed like code (never auto-accepted in CI), flake killed at the source (disable animations, pin viewport/device-scale, freeze fonts, mask volatile regions, SMALL tolerance), a harness assertion PREFERRED to a snapshot for semantic properties, and @axe-core/playwright on the SAME browser run for real-layout a11y (Book 16 §3.2) — the smallest, most maintenance-heavy band and the ROADMAP cut-order fallback-to-manual (the REQUIREMENT doesn't fall back, Book 16 §3.5). CI wiring maps layers to the project posture (unit+component = fast per-PR gate; integration+visual = heavier matrix on-label/milestone/post-merge), with DETERMINISM non-negotiable (no wall-clock/random/network) and golden updates gated by review. PROVENANCE tie-back: the WHOLE toolchain (Vitest MIT/antfu, jsdom, @playwright/test 1.61.1 Apache-2.0/Microsoft/US, @axe-core/playwright, axe-core) is DEV-TIER — in devDependencies, EXCLUDED from the shipped-tree scan (Book 03 §3.3/§3.4; the M0-4 gate implements the runtime-vs-dev split with axe's MPL the worked example); only runtime deps face the US-origin bar. Seven-leg test-authoring checklist (pure->unit / interaction->harness / zoneless-settling-awaited / composition->integration-with-fallback / visual->snapshot+axe / deterministic / dev-tier-provenance); definition_of_done runs legs 1-4+6 as its build/test gate, the visual leg joins at milestone/e2e, leg 7 is the M0-4 gate. Backs the add_test skill. Bleeding edge: the Vitest default is new+moving (don't hard-pin a minor; the library-vs-application ng-packagr test-wiring is an OPEN verify-at-M0 question); jsdom-vs-browser-mode is a real fork (jsdom for the fat middle, browser mode for layout/visual); the Karma->Vitest migration schematic is experimental+MOOT for greenfield Caelum (a Book 20 adoption concern); SR automation still not a dependable gate (Book 16 §5 — don't let green Vitest+Playwright masquerade as full a11y); visual-regression flake is the perennial tax. Honest status: NO test infrastructure yet (no component code — M0 hasn't started); this defines the tooling the M0-M4 components are tested with, runner choice provisional-pending-M0. All runner/toolchain facts grounded in research/notes/angular-22-testing.md (frontier, cited as a research note never as a Book §).

books.title: Performance & Bundle Budgets
key_concepts: performance is a BUDGETED, GATED dimension of 'is done' (like a11y in Book 16) — set the number, measure against it, FAIL CI when crossed; the standing rule is PROFILED, NOT GUESSED, so every quantitative claim is a candidate EXPERIMENT not a memorized figure

THE distinctive Caelum insight: TWO size gates, not one — angular.json budgets gate the FORGE APPLICATION build (esbuild), but they do NOT measure the ng-packagr LIBRARY output, so the shipped cae-* package needs its OWN per-secondary-entry-point size gate (source-map-explorer/size-limit); a library is not an application (Book 01 §2.2)

angular.json BUDGETS (the app gate): types initial/anyComponentStyle/bundle/allScript/all/anyScript; thresholds maximumWarning/maximumError + baseline+% form; the GATE = warning proceeds but error FAILS ng build; type-level defaults initial 500kb/1mb + anyComponentStyle 2kb/4kb (research note)

TREE-SHAKING is a PACKAGING discipline decided at build time (a consumer can't retrofit it): ship APF/FESM2022 with 'sideEffects': false (the highest-leverage package.json line), ONE secondary entry point per component/adapter (so importing cae-select never pulls cae-data-grid — the literal 'adopt component-by-component' mechanism), partial compilation, tree-shakable provide*()/providedIn services (already the Book 12 §3.4 adapter DI shape)

LAZY LOADING: @defer deferrable views split STANDALONE deps into separate chunks (triggers on viewport/interaction/idle/hover/timer/when + prefetch, @placeholder/@loading/@error) — the right tool for Caelum's HEAVIEST artifacts (the D3 chart, Lexical editor, TanStack grid — Books 13-15); + standalone lazy routes (loadComponent) for Forge screens

CHANGE-DETECTION cost: zoneless + OnPush + signals (v22 default, Book 01 §3.2) means a cae-* re-checks only when its own signals change; PROVE it with the Angular DevTools profiler's 'Change detection' checkbox (skipped OnPush subtrees GREY OUT); virtual scroll (Book 10 §3.3, the R1 lever) bounds the DOM independent of dataset size (fixed itemSize shippable; autosize experimental)

PROFILING instruments (all dev-tier): Angular DevTools Profiler (bar chart of CD cycles, flame graph, 'Change detection' greying, JSON export for before/after) + Chrome DevTools Performance via ng.enableProfiling() (dev-mode); the rule: profile FIRST, optimize the MEASURED hotspot, re-profile — never optimize an unprofiled hunch (the premature-optimization guard, backs optimize_loop/profile_subsystem)

the DEV-TIER analysis toolchain (@angular/build esbuild pipeline + ng build --stats-json → esbuild analyzer as the zero-dep first-party path; source-map-explorer Apache-2.0 builder-agnostic; webpack-bundle-analyzer is webpack-only = fit mismatch) is never shipped, so Book 03 §3.3's runtime-vs-dev rule relaxes its license/origin bar

six-leg perf checklist (app-budget-set / library-size-gate-set / tree-shakable-packaging / heavy-deps-deferred / CD-bounded-and-proven / profiled-not-guessed); definition_of_done's perf gate enforces legs 1-2+5-6; COMPLETES Volume IV

boundaries kept clean: Book 17 owns test TOOLING (grid-at-scale tests) → Book 18 owns their perf BUDGETS (Book 17 §5 handoff); Book 19 owns HOW to publish the APF → Book 18 owns WHY (bundle cost); Book 10 §3.6 forward-ref'd here for 'profile at realistic scale'

honest gaps NAMED not covered: SSR/incremental hydration (@defer hydrate triggers), runtime Web-Vitals/Lighthouse-CI budget gates, router preloading, NgOptimizedImage/font/critical-CSS — flagged so silence isn't mistaken for coverage; all facts grounded in research/notes/angular-22-performance.md never memory or a Book §
books.summary: The third book of Volume IV — the PERFORMANCE dimension of 'is done', and the book that COMPLETES the Quality volume. The project rule governs it: performance is PROFILED, NOT GUESSED, so performance is a BUDGETED, GATED dimension (set the number, measure, FAIL CI when crossed — the same stance as Book 16's axe gate) and every quantitative claim is a candidate EXPERIMENT, not a memorized figure. Four levers. (1) BUNDLE-SIZE GATES — and the distinctive Caelum insight the research surfaced: TWO gates, not one. angular.json BUDGETS (types initial/anyComponentStyle/bundle/…, thresholds maximumWarning/maximumError + baseline+%, the gate = warning proceeds but error FAILS ng build, defaults initial 500kb/1mb + anyComponentStyle 2kb/4kb) gate the FORGE APPLICATION build — but budgets are an application-build feature and do NOT measure ng-packagr LIBRARY output, so the shipped cae-* package needs its OWN per-secondary-entry-point size gate (source-map-explorer/size-limit on the dist), because a library is not an application (Book 01 §2.2); conflating them is the trap. (2) TREE-SHAKING as a PACKAGING discipline decided at build time: ship APF/FESM2022 with 'sideEffects': false (the highest-leverage package.json line), ONE secondary entry point per component/adapter (the literal 'adopt component-by-component' mechanism — importing cae-select never pulls cae-data-grid, the exact shape Material uses), partial compilation, and tree-shakable provide*()/providedIn services (already Book 12 §3.4's adapter-DI shape, so the adapter pattern is already tree-shaking-friendly). (3) LAZY LOADING: @defer deferrable views split STANDALONE deps into separate chunks (triggers on viewport/interaction/idle/hover/timer/when + prefetch; @placeholder/@loading/@error) — the right tool for Caelum's HEAVIEST artifacts, the chart/editor/grid adapters (Books 13-15); plus standalone lazy routes (loadComponent) for Forge screens; the adapter's DI boundary composes naturally with deferring the heavy impl. (4) CHANGE-DETECTION cost: zoneless + OnPush + signals (the v22 default, Book 01 §3.2) means a cae-* re-checks only when its own signals change, PROVEN via the Angular DevTools profiler's 'Change detection' checkbox (skipped OnPush subtrees GREY OUT); virtual scroll (Book 10 §3.3, the R1 lever) bounds the DOM independent of dataset size (fixed itemSize is shippable, variable-height autosize is experimental), so grid-at-scale = virtual scroll bounds the DOM + OnPush/signals bounds the CD over it — where Book 10's R1 decision gets its EVIDENCE (profile at realistic scale). PROFILING instruments (all dev-tier): the Angular DevTools Profiler (bar chart of CD cycles, flame graph, the 'Change detection' greying, JSON export for before/after) and Chrome DevTools Performance via ng.enableProfiling() (dev-mode); the discipline is profile-first-optimize-the-measured-hotspot-re-profile, never an unprofiled hunch (the premature-optimization guard, backing optimize_loop/profile_subsystem). The dev-tier analysis toolchain (@angular/build's esbuild pipeline + ng build --stats-json → esbuild analyzer as the zero-added-dependency first-party path; source-map-explorer 2.5.3 Apache-2.0, builder-agnostic; webpack-bundle-analyzer webpack-only = a fit mismatch for the esbuild default) is never shipped, so Book 03 §3.3's runtime-vs-dev rule relaxes its license/origin bar. Six-leg perf checklist (app-budget-set / library-size-gate-set / tree-shakable-packaging / heavy-deps-deferred / CD-bounded-and-proven / profiled-not-guessed); definition_of_done's perf gate enforces legs 1-2+5-6. Boundaries kept clean: Book 17 owns the test TOOLING (the grid-at-scale tests) and handed their perf BUDGETS to this book (Book 17 §5); Book 19 owns HOW to publish the tree-shakable APF, this book owns WHY it matters (bundle cost) — a why-vs-how split; Book 10 §3.6 forward-referenced this book for 'profile at realistic scale'. Bleeding edge / honestly-named gaps (NOT covered, so silence isn't mistaken for coverage): incremental hydration (@defer's hydrate triggers — the SSR dimension), runtime Web-Vitals/Lighthouse-CI budget gates (the complement to bundle-size gates; INP is what a component library most affects), router preloading strategies, NgOptimizedImage/font/critical-CSS, and autosize virtual scroll (still experimental). Honest status: NO perf infrastructure yet (no component code/Forge app/CI — M0 hasn't started); this defines the budgets/gates the M0-M4 work wires, the library size gate (leg 2) is a genuine design decision for M0-4, and all numbers are EXP-NN seeds. Backs the profile_subsystem/optimize_loop skills; all v22 performance facts grounded in research/notes/angular-22-performance.md (frontier — bundle budgets, APF/sideEffects/secondary entry points, @defer, the profiler; cited as a research note never as a Book §), which cross-references angular-22-platform.md for the zoneless/OnPush facts. COMPLETES Volume IV — Quality.

books.title: Packaging, Versioning & Distribution
key_concepts: OPENS Volume V (Distribution & Adoption): this book is the HOW-TO-PUBLISH; its sibling Book 18 was the WHY-the-shape-matters (bundle cost) — the same why/how boundary as 16↔17 (a11y verification vs test tooling) and 17↔18 (test tooling vs perf budgets); Book 20 (next) completes the volume AND the 20-book library with the PrimeNG→Caelum migration

THE KEYSTONE: a published package carries TWO ORTHOGONAL PROVENANCES — npm/SLSA BUILD-origin provenance (which repo/commit/CI job produced the tarball, via OIDC + Sigstore) vs Caelum's US-ORIGIN attestation (who authored/owns the code + its transitive tree, D-05/D-10, Book 03 §2.3); neither implies the other, and a complete release carries BOTH — the build provenance recorded in the registry/transparency log, the US-origin attestation inside the tarball; 'we have npm provenance so we are US-origin-clean' is the trap the book exists to prevent (provenance is a separate orthogonal axis — neither necessary nor sufficient for the US-origin claim)

LIBRARY BUILD: the v22 library builder is @angular/build:ng-packagr (always AOT, always PARTIAL compilation — code not tied to a runtime version, so the CONSUMER compiles Caelum against its own Angular), emitting the Angular Package Format (FESM2022 + bundled types/ + a generated package.json); the publish flow is ng build <lib> --configuration production → cd dist/<lib> → npm publish (the dist IS the package); ng-packagr 22.0.0 MIT is a DEV/build-tier tool judged by the relaxed dev bar (Book 03 §3.3) (research note)

CONSUMER CONTRACT: APF's exports map keys each entry point by types + default ONLY (CORRECTION the research caught: esm2022 is NOT a v22 exports condition — a blog myth; module/typings are deprecated fallbacks); SECONDARY ENTRY POINTS = one subfolder with its own ng-package.json (can be {}) per component/adapter → caelum/select, caelum/data-grid, each independently importable + tree-shakable (Material's shape) = the package-level meaning of 'adopt component-by-component' (Book 18 §3.3 owns WHY it tree-shakes); a sass condition exposes Book 04's theme assets

DEPENDENCIES + VERSIONING: @angular/* go in peerDependencies NOT dependencies (else a duplicate-Angular-instance bug breaks the consumer); angular.dev prescribes NO range syntax — the OBSERVED convention from Angular's own CDK/Material (22.0.2) is the multi-major caret-OR ^22.0.0 || ^23.0.0 (Material pins @angular/cdk EXACTLY = one unit); adopt Angular semver (breaking only in a major, ≥2-major/≥12-month deprecation window) + a lockstep Caelum-major-per-Angular-major; ship an ng-update collection (migrations/version/factory) for breaking cae-* changes — DISTINCT from Book 20's p-*→cae-* migration codemods (research note)

DISTRIBUTION with two attestations: publish from GitHub Actions via npm Trusted Publishing (OIDC id-token:write, ubuntu-latest, NO stored token) → provenance AUTO-generated for a public repo/package (no --provenance flag) = SLSA Build Level 2, Sigstore-signed (Fulcio ~10-min cert + Rekor transparency log), npm badge, verify via npm audit signatures; SEPARATELY ship Caelum's US-origin attestation (the walked runtime tree, Book 03 §3.3/§3.4) IN the tarball; a release is done only when DOUBLY ATTESTED + SIZE-GATED (the Book 18 §3.2 shipped-library per-entry-point gate, an M0-4 bespoke CI step)

backs D-06 (Caelum is a downloadable library) + definition_of_done's PROVENANCE gate (the US-origin scan of the SHIPPED runtime tree); the shipped-library size gate + the US-origin-attestation emitter are M0-4 design tasks (no platform equivalent); honest gaps NAMED: the US-origin attestation FORMAT is a Caelum invention (SLSA standardizes build-origin, not tree-origin), manual --provenance on private packages + the SLSA predicate version emitted today are unconfirmed, and the ng-update field-set is from the undated angular-cli spec repo — verify at M0; all v22 packaging facts grounded in research/notes/angular-22-packaging.md, never memory or a Book §
books.summary: The FIRST book of Volume V (Distribution & Adoption) — it OPENS the volume. Volume IV made 'looks done' mean 'is done'; Volume V makes a finished library REACH a consuming team. This book is the HOW-TO-PUBLISH; its sibling Book 18 was the WHY-the-shape-matters (bundle cost) — keep the boundary clean, the mirror of 16↔17 and 17↔18: Book 18 §3.3 owns WHY a tree-shakable secondary-entry-point package is worth shipping, this book owns HOW to build/version/ship it. THE KEYSTONE the research surfaced: a published package carries TWO ORTHOGONAL PROVENANCES. npm/SLSA BUILD-origin provenance cryptographically links the tarball to its source repo/commit/build-workflow/CI-job (signed via the runner's OIDC token, logged in a public transparency ledger) — it answers 'was this really built from this commit by this pipeline'. Caelum's US-ORIGIN attestation (D-05/D-10, Book 03 §2.3, 'the tree is the unit') is the ORTHOGONAL axis — who authored/owns the code and its transitive runtime tree. Neither implies the other, so a Caelum release ships BOTH: npm/SLSA provenance (from the CI publish) AND a US-origin attestation manifest packaged INTO the tarball; conflating them ('we have provenance so we are US-origin-clean') is the trap. Five moves. (1) LIBRARY BUILD — the v22 library builder is @angular/build:ng-packagr (distinct from the esbuild application builder = the two-builder proof of Book 01 §2.2's library-is-not-an-application), always AOT, always PARTIAL compilation (code not tied to a runtime version → the consumer compiles it), emitting the Angular Package Format (FESM2022 + bundled types/ + generated package.json); ng build <lib> --configuration production → cd dist/<lib> → npm publish (the dist IS the package); ng-packagr 22.0.0 MIT runs at build time = dev-tier (Book 03 §3.3). (2) CONSUMER CONTRACT — the exports map keys each entry point by types+default ONLY (esm2022 is NOT a v22 condition, correcting a blog myth; module/typings are deprecated fallbacks), fesm2022/*.mjs, bundled types/*.d.ts, 'sideEffects': false; SECONDARY ENTRY POINTS are the 'component-by-component' mechanism at the package level — one subfolder with its own ng-package.json (can be as simple as {}) per component/adapter yields caelum/select, caelum/data-grid, each independently importable + tree-shakable (Material's shape); Book 18 §3.3 owns WHY this tree-shakes, here is HOW it is authored; a sass condition exposes Book 04's theme stylesheets. (3) DEPENDENCIES — @angular/* as peerDependencies NOT dependencies (dependencies risks a duplicate-Angular-instance break); angular.dev prescribes no range syntax, so Caelum follows the OBSERVED convention from Angular's own CDK/Material — the multi-major caret-OR ^22.0.0 || ^23.0.0 (Material pins @angular/cdk exactly = released as one unit); the three adapter libs (grid/charts/editor, Books 13-15) stay behind their adapters as OPTIONAL/lazy peers so the core's US-origin tree stays minimal. (4) VERSIONING — inherit Angular semver (breaking only in a major, backward-compatible minors, ≥2-major/≥12-month deprecation window; major ~every 6 months, 6mo Active + 12mo LTS) and cut a lockstep Caelum-MAJOR-per-Angular-major (the Material/CDK discipline); ship an ng-update migration collection (the package.json ng-update block → migrations path → per-entry version+factory) so a breaking cae-* change rewrites consumer templates on ng update — DISTINCT from Book 20's p-*→cae-* codemods (ng-update upgrades a team already ON Caelum; Book 20 moves a team FROM PrimeNG). (5) DISTRIBUTION — publish from GitHub Actions via npm Trusted Publishing (OIDC id-token:write, a GitHub-hosted ubuntu-latest runner, NO stored npm token) so provenance is AUTO-generated for the public repo/package (no --provenance flag) = SLSA Build Level 2, Sigstore-signed (Fulcio short-lived cert + Rekor transparency log), a verifiable npm badge, consumers check with npm audit signatures; SEPARATELY emit Caelum's US-origin attestation (the transitive runtime tree's license+origin, Book 03 §3.3/§3.4) into the tarball. The RELEASE CHECKLIST (wiring into definition_of_done's provenance gate) — built correctly (ng-packagr, production, partial, per-entry exports/sideEffects/one-secondary-entry-point-each), peer/version-correct (peer ^22||^23, nothing Angular in dependencies, lockstep version, ng-update present for breaking changes), SIZE-GATED (the Book 18 §3.2 shipped-library per-entry-point gate — the bespoke M0-4 CI step angular.json budgets do not cover), and DOUBLY ATTESTED (npm/SLSA build provenance + the US-origin manifest, both green). §4 gives the load-bearing config (ng-package.json, an illustrative emitted dist/package.json exports+peerDependencies+ng-update, the trusted-publishing release.yml, a us-origin.attestation.json sketch) with a standing warning to VERIFY exact field sets against the live v22 workspace at M0, never hand-copy as gospel. AI integration draws bright lines: never fabricate version-specific packaging config (builder strings/exports conditions/peer ranges/ng-update fields/provenance flags are all post-cutoff — ground in the note or verify; the note already caught the esm2022 and prescribed-peer-range traps), treat the two-provenance rule as a review invariant (npm provenance must not stand in for the US-origin scan), author ng-update migrations alongside breaking changes, and guard the size gate as release-blocking. Realizes D-06 (downloadable library) and backs definition_of_done's provenance gate; the shipped-library size gate + the US-origin-attestation emitter are M0-4 design tasks with no platform equivalent. Honest gaps NAMED: the US-origin attestation FORMAT is a Caelum invention (no standard — SLSA covers build-origin, not tree-origin), monorepo/many-@caelum-package publishing, mechanical deprecation-window tooling, manual --provenance on private packages + the SLSA predicate version + the ng-update field-set (from the undated angular-cli spec) all to verify at M0. All v22 packaging facts grounded in research/notes/angular-22-packaging.md (frontier — ng-packagr/APF, exports/secondary entry points, peer deps/lockstep, semver/deprecation, npm provenance/SLSA; cited as a research note, never as a Book §). OPENS Volume V; Book 20 (Migration & Adoption) completes it and the library.

books.title: Migration & Adoption
key_concepts: CLOSES Volume V and COMPLETES the 20-book library (Book 20 is the FINAL book); it is the last-mile that publishing (Book 19) exists to serve — how a team actually leaves PrimeNG for Caelum component-by-component without a rewrite; realizes D-06 (app-agnostic library a team ADOPTS, not an in-place migration) and generalizes D-03/Book 12's adapter isolation from one library to a whole one being removed

THE BOUNDARY with Book 19 §3.4 (stated in both directions): ng-update migrations upgrade a team ALREADY ON Caelum across Caelum MAJORS (same-package, version-gated); the p-*->cae-* codemods here move a team FROM PrimeNG (cross-package) — so the from-PrimeNG transform is a STANDALONE `ng generate caelum:migrate-from-primeng` Schematic, NEVER an ng-update migration (the load-bearing architectural conclusion, grounded in how the three Schematics surfaces ng-add/ng-generate/ng-update are defined)
